params.name: Scrambled Eggs on Toast - Shopping List
params.content:
# Scrambled Eggs on Toast - Shopping List

## Essential Ingredients
- [ ] Eggs (4 large)
- [ ] Bread (for toasting, 2–4 slices)
- [ ] Butter (for cooking and spreading)
- [ ] Salt
- [ ] Black pepper

## Optional Extras
- [ ] Milk or cream (a splash for fluffier eggs)
- [ ] Chives or fresh herbs (for garnish)
- [ ] Cheese (cheddar or your preference, for mixing in)

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFK" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFK</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

<event>
{
  "queue": "dxn:queue:data:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG5",
  "item": {
    "id": "01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTGA",
    "created": "2026-01-28T10:15:00.000Z",
    "sender": {
      "email": "receipts@britishairways.com"
    },
    "blocks": [
      {
        "_tag": "text",
        "text": "From: receipts@britishairways.com
Subject: Your British Airways booking confirmation - BA287

Dear Mr. Smith,

Thank you for booking with British Airways. Your flight reservation is confirmed.

BOOKING REFERENCE: XK7M2P

FLIGHT DETAILS
─────────────────────────────────────
Flight: BA287
Date: 15 February 2026
Route: San Francisco (SFO) → London Heathrow (LHR)
Departure: 17:45 PST
Arrival: 12:15 GMT (+1 day)
Class: Economy

PASSENGER
─────────────────────────────────────
John Smith

PAYMENT SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────
Base fare:              £485.00
Taxes and fees:         £127.50
─────────────────────────────────────
TOTAL PAID:             £612.50

Payment method: Visa ending in 4521
Transaction date: 28 January 2026

Please keep this email for your records.

Kind regards,
British Airways"
      }
    ]
  },
  "cursor": "1"
}
</event>

response.delta:
user has received an email from British Airways with a flight booking confirmation. I need to:

1. Create an

"Expenses" markdown document (since there are no artifacts yet)
2. Add the expense from

this email to the document
3. Add the artifact to the project

Let me extract the expense details:
- Flight from

San Francisco to London Heathrow (BA287)
- Date: 2026-02-15 (flight date)
- Transaction

date: 2026-01-28
- Total: £612.50

I'll use the flight
date (2026-02-15) as the expense date since that's when the service occurs.
I see a British Airways flight booking confirmation. Let me create the
Expenses document and log this expense.

tools.name: add-artifact

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFK" name="Expense tracking">

      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.

  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100

</blueprint>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV5M" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

# State

- 3 raw eggs (in fridge)
- 1 knob of butter (in fridge)
- Salt (in pantry)
- Pepper (in pantry)
- 1 frying pan (in cabinet)
- 1 spatula (in drawer)
- 1 bowl (in cabinet)
- 1 fork (in drawer)
- 1 plate (in cabinet)

# Action Log

_(no actions yet)_

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>

</example>

2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>

</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV5M" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients and tools (eggs, butter, salt, pepper, pan, spatula, bowl, fork) <!-- id=task_1 -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and beat them <!-- id=task_2 -->
  - **DONE**: Heat the pan and melt butter <!-- id=task_3 -->
  - **DONE**: Pour beaten eggs into the pan <!-- id=task_4 -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Stir and scramble the eggs until cooked <!-- id=task_5 -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=task_6 -->
  - **TODO**: Plate and serve the scrambled eggs <!-- id=task_7 -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNVB2">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV5M</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>
Go

content.text:
The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, create tasks for making scrambled eggs
2. Create a markdown document to simulate the plan execution
3. Simulate actions one by one, updating the document

Let me start by creating the tasks and the initial markdown document.
Let's get started! I'll break down the scrambled eggs recipe into tasks and begin simulating the process.

tasks.title: Gather ingredients and tools (eggs, butter, salt, pepper, pan, spatula, bowl, fork)
Crack eggs into a bowl and beat them
Heat the pan and melt butter
Pour beaten eggs into the pan
Stir and scramble the eggs until cooked
Season with salt and pepper
Plate and serve the scrambled eggs

# State

- 3 empty eggshells (in trash)
- Salt (on counter)
- Pepper (on counter)
- 1 frying pan (on stove, medium heat, contains raw egg mixture in melted butter)
- 1 spatula (on counter)
- 1 bowl (on counter, empty, residue of egg)
- 1 fork (on counter, used)
- 1 plate (on counter)

# Action Log

1. Gathered 3 raw eggs from the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Retrieved salt and pepper from the pantry.
4. Placed frying pan on the stove.
5. Took spatula from drawer and placed on counter.
6. Got bowl from cabinet and placed on counter.
7. Got fork from drawer and placed on counter.
8. Got plate from cabinet and placed on counter.
9. Cracked egg #1 into the bowl, discarded shell.
10. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
11. Cracked egg #3 into the bowl, discarded shell.
12. Beat the eggs with the fork until well combined.
13. Turned stove burner to medium heat.
14. Placed butter knob into the frying pan.
15. Waited for butter to melt and coat the pan evenly.
16. Poured beaten eggs from the bowl into the hot buttered pan.
17. Eggs began to set around the edges.

3. Retrieved salt and pepper from the pantry.

10. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
11. Cracked egg #3 into the bowl, discarded shell.
12. Beat the eggs with the fork until well combined.
13. Turned stove burner to medium heat.
14. Placed butter knob into the frying pan.

18. Picked up spatula and began gently stirring the eggs.
19. Pushed eggs from edges toward the center, forming soft curds.
20. Continued folding and stirring for ~2 minutes.
21. Eggs are now soft, fluffy curds — just cooked through. Removed pan from direct heat.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When creating a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV5M" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients and tools (eggs, butter, salt, pepper, pan, spatula, bowl, fork) <!-- id=task_1 -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and beat them <!-- id=task_2 -->
  - **DONE**: Heat the pan and melt butter <!-- id=task_3 -->
  - **DONE**: Pour beaten eggs into the pan <!-- id=task_4 -->
  - **DONE**: Stir and scramble the eggs until cooked <!-- id=task_5 -->
  - **DONE**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=task_6 -->
  - **DONE**: Plate and serve the scrambled eggs <!-- id=task_7 -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNVB2">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You read, write & create markdown documents.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV5M</dxn>

</object>

# State

- 3 empty eggshells (in trash)
- Salt (on counter)
- Pepper (on counter)
- 1 frying pan (on stove, medium heat, contains soft scrambled eggs — just cooked)
- 1 spatula (in hand, used for stirring)
- 1 bowl (on counter, empty)
- 1 fork (on counter, used)
- 1 plate (on counter)

# Action Log

1. Gathered 3 raw eggs from the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Retrieved salt and pepper from the pantry.
4. Placed frying pan on the stove.
5. Took spatula from drawer and placed on counter.
6. Got bowl from cabinet and placed on counter.
7. Got fork from drawer and placed on counter.
8. Got plate from cabinet and placed on counter.
9. Cracked egg #1 into the bowl, discarded shell.
10. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
11. Cracked egg #3 into the bowl, discarded shell.
12. Beat the eggs with the fork until well combined.
13. Turned stove burner to medium heat.
14. Placed butter knob into the frying pan.
15. Waited for butter to melt and coat the pan evenly.
16. Poured beaten eggs from the bowl into the hot buttered pan.
17. Eggs began to set around the edges.
18. Picked up spatula and began gently stirring the eggs.
19. Pushed eggs from edges toward the center, forming soft curds.
20. Continued folding and stirring for ~2 minutes.
21. Eggs are now soft, fluffy curds — just cooked through. Removed pan from direct heat.

2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Retrieved salt and pepper from the pantry.
4. Placed frying pan on the stove.
5. Took spatula from drawer and placed on counter.

15. Waited for butter to melt and coat the pan evenly.

params.content:
# State

- 3 empty eggshells (in trash)
- Salt (on counter, used)
- Pepper (on counter, used)
- 1 frying pan (on stove, off heat, empty, needs washing)
- 1 spatula (on counter, needs washing)
- 1 bowl (on counter, empty, needs washing)
- 1 fork (on counter, needs washing)
- 1 plate (on table, contains a serving of seasoned scrambled eggs) ✅

# Action Log

1. Gathered 3 raw eggs from the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Retrieved salt and pepper from the pantry.
4. Placed frying pan on the stove.
5. Took spatula from drawer and placed on counter.
6. Got bowl from cabinet and placed on counter.
7. Got fork from drawer and placed on counter.
8. Got plate from cabinet and placed on counter.
9. Cracked egg #1 into the bowl, discarded shell.
10. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
11. Cracked egg #3 into the bowl, discarded shell.
12. Beat the eggs with the fork until well combined.
13. Turned stove burner to medium heat.
14. Placed butter knob into the frying pan.
15. Waited for butter to melt and coat the pan evenly.
16. Poured beaten eggs from the bowl into the hot buttered pan.
17. Eggs began to set around the edges.
18. Picked up spatula and began gently stirring the eggs.
19. Pushed eggs from edges toward the center, forming soft curds.
20. Continued folding and stirring for ~2 minutes.
21. Eggs are now soft, fluffy curds — just cooked through. Removed pan from direct heat.
22. Added a pinch of salt over the scrambled eggs.
23. Added a few grinds of black pepper over the scrambled eggs.
24. Gave a final gentle stir to distribute the seasoning.
25. Used spatula to transfer scrambled eggs from pan onto the plate.
26. Turned off the stove burner.
27. Placed plate on the table. Scrambled eggs are ready to serve! 🍳
